cards.text: A {{c1::constant expression}} is an expression that can be evaluated at compile-time.
Expressions involving only {{c1::literals}} and {{c2::const variables}} are constant expressions.
A {{c1::compile-time constant}} is a constant whose value is known at compile-time.
A {{c1::runtime constant}} is a constant whose value is not known until runtime (e.g., initialized from user input).

cards.question: Which of these are constant expressions?
cards.answer:
x and y are constant expressions (compile-time)
w is NOT a constant expression (runtime constant)
cards.explanation: x and y can be evaluated at compile-time, but w depends on user input which is only known at runtime.

cards.question: Is this a constant expression?
cards.answer: Yes, both x and y are constant expressions that can be evaluated at compile-time.
cards.explanation: Since x is a compile-time constant, the expression x * 2 + 1 can be fully evaluated during compilation.

cards.front: What is the difference between a compile-time constant and a runtime constant?
cards.back:
• Compile-time constant: value is known during compilation
  • Runtime constant: value is determined during program execution
  • Both use const keyword but serve different purposes

cards.front: Can a non-const variable be part of a constant expression?
cards.back: No. Only literals, const variables (if they are compile-time constants), and constexpr variables can be part of constant expressions.

cards.explanation:
Constant expressions enable:
  • Compile-time optimization (better performance)
  • Array sizes (must be compile-time constants)
  • Template arguments (must be compile-time constants)
  • Case labels in switch statements
The compiler can evaluate them once during compilation instead of repeatedly at runtime.
examples:
const int size{ 5 };
int arr[size];  // OK: size is constant expression
